coredata.dc:title: Readily soluble cellulose-based fluorescent probes for the detection and removal of Fe3+ ion
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Yanqin
Cheng, Xinjian
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose is an economical, biodegradable, widely available, and eco-friendly natural macromolecule. But its utilization has been restricted due to its insolubility in water and common organic solvents. In this work, soluble fluorescent probes based on cellulose were synthesized. Firstly, the primary hydroxyl group in glucose units was reacted with SOCl2 to introduce Cl and obtain chloro-cellulose (Cell-Cl). This operation breaks down the regular structure and hydrogen bonding of the original cellulose, enabling it to dissolve in DMSO. Secondly, the Cell-Cl reacted with CS2 and 2-mercaptobenzothiazole to obtain a cellulose-based macromolecular RAFT reagent (Cell-CTA). Finally, the fluorescent monomers which bears -C=C- and naphthalimide, and methacrylic acid (MAA) were grafted onto the main chain of cellulose through RAFT polymerization. Thus, cellulose-based readily soluble macromolecular fluorescent probes were obtained. The cellulose-based probes can specifically recognize Fe3+ in pure water and can be recycled and regenerated. Additionally, the cellulose-based probes exhibit remarkable adsorption and separation properties for Fe3+ ions. The modification of cellulose decreases its crystallinity and introduces hydrophilic groups and fluorophores, which enables cellulose to be soluble in both pure water and the organic solvent DMSO. This work expands the application range of cellulose-based copolymers.

head.ce:title: Readily soluble cellulose-based fluorescent probes for the detection and removal of Fe3+ ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan, China, 430073

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose is an economical, biodegradable, widely available, and eco-friendly natural macromolecule. But its utilization has been restricted due to its insolubility in water and common organic solvents. In this work, soluble fluorescent probes based on cellulose were synthesized. Firstly, the primary hydroxyl group in glucose units was reacted with SOCl2 to introduce Cl and obtain chloro-cellulose (Cell-Cl). This operation breaks down the regular structure and hydrogen bonding of the original cellulose, enabling it to dissolve in DMSO. Secondly, the Cell-Cl reacted with CS2 and 2-mercaptobenzothiazole to obtain a cellulose-based macromolecular RAFT reagent (Cell-CTA). Finally, the fluorescent monomers which bears -C=C- and naphthalimide, and methacrylic acid (MAA) were grafted onto the main chain of cellulose through RAFT polymerization. Thus, cellulose-based readily soluble macromolecular fluorescent probes were obtained. The cellulose-based probes can specifically recognize Fe3+ in pure water and can be recycled and regenerated. Additionally, the cellulose-based probes exhibit remarkable adsorption and separation properties for Fe3+ ions. The modification of cellulose decreases its crystallinity and introduces hydrophilic groups and fluorophores, which enables cellulose to be soluble in both pure water and the organic solvent DMSO. This work expands the application range of cellulose-based copolymers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data and samples are available in the author's Lab.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose is the most abundant and widely distributed natural polymer, its outstanding merits is the low cost and biodegradability [1]. It exhibits low safety risk to human beings, animals, and the environment, due to the good biocompatibility. Cellulose is typically derived from wood, hemp, cotton, flax and agricultural waste [2], making it a natural biomass resource [3]. In view of the chemical structure, cellulose is a kind of polysaccharide compound [4], consisting of many glucose moieties connected by β-1, 4-glucoside bonds [5]. The hydroxyl functional group (-OH) in cellulose could react with other compounds readily. Many reactions could be used to modify the original cellulose, such as esterification, etherification, and other reactions. As reported in published literatures [6]: C6 ≫ C3 > C2. The highest reactivity is observed at the C6 position compared to other positions.

Cellulose molecules are structurally uniformly constructed and distributed, with each glucose unit containing three hydroxyl groups, which allows for the extensive presence of hydrogen bonds. Due to the existence of intermolecular hydrogen bonds in cellulose [7], cellulose is insoluble in water and most organic solvents. When the hydrogen bond between cellulose was broken up, cellulose became soluble in many solvents. Compared with others' works, the main advantage of the synthetic pathway is that cellulose can be modified in common organic solvents throughout the entire process without using Cu-ammonia-Cu(NH3)4(OH)2 solution, ionic liquid [8], or sodium hydroxide/urea aqueous solution [9]. The problem of insolubility was solved by dissolving cellulose in Cu-ammonia-Cu(NH3)4(OH)2 solution, ionic liquid, and sodium hydroxide/urea aqueous solution. However, due to the complexity of the composition of these cellulose solutions, it is not convenient to further utilize them for producing functional polymers in this work. Therefore, developing pure water or pure organic solvent systems is of great significance for fully utilizing cellulose-based materials. The hydroxyl groups in the cellulose structure have a certain ability of ion exchange and could interact with cations or anions in solution [10]. This ion exchange makes cellulose adsorb, remove or release specific ions in the environment, with potential applications in environmental remediation and water treatment [11].

Heavy metal ions are widely found in nature and widely used in industry. Fe3+ plays a key role in organisms under appropriate conditions, such as participating in oxygen transport and storage, cell metabolism, and enzyme catalyzed reactions [12]. However, Fe3+ is bioaccumulated and non-degradable ion, excessive Fe3+ will cause serious harm to organisms. Fe3+ becomes toxic to proteins and enzymes and interferes with the normal cell functions, thus disrupting the body's immune function [13]. High Fe3+ ion intake or excessive absorption leads to iron poisoning. Excessive Fe3+ ion in wastewater often cause pollution in groundwater and surrounding water body. Thus Fe3+ ion contaminates human daily drinking water, and poses serious impacts on human health [14].

In recent years, there have been a lot of reports on the detection of heavy metal ions. According to the literatures [15], there are many methods used to detect Fe3+, such as atomic absorption spectrometry, X-ray fluorescence spectrometry, UV–visible spectrophotometry, inductively coupled plasma method and so on. But these methods have some shortcomings, such as high expense, complicated operation and pre-processing, not green enough problems [16]. Fluorescence method has become a good ions detection method because of its high sensitivity [17], real-time monitoring, quantitative analysis and other advantages [18], it is especially suitable for research and application fields requiring high sensitivity and specific detection [19]. Compared with physical methods (filtration, ion exchange, reverse osmosis) and chemical methods (electrochemical pretreatment, precipitation) used to remove heavy metal ions in water [20], the adsorption method could use different adsorbents, and has the advantages of high efficiency, low cost, environmental friendly and reversible [21].

Reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (RAFT) is a reversible free radical polymerization technique. RAFT reagents react with monomers to form intermediates with active groups, enabling precise molecular weight design by adjusting the concentration of reactants and reaction conditions [22]. By adjusting the chain length and proportion of different chain segments in the polymer, we can tailor its structure and properties to achieve a desired combination of hydrophobic-hydrophobic and hydrophilic-hydrophobic monomers. In other words, compared with conventional free radical polymerization, RAFT polymerization has better controllability, allowing accurate molecular weight control and narrow molecular weight distribution [23].

Graft copolymerization is one of the most widely used methods to synthesize new polymeric materials and to modify polymers. Macromolecular RAFT reagents are those with higher molecular weight and can be used to synthesize co-polymers. This reagent is an important species for the synthesis of grafting copolymers by generating radicals on the main chain of the polymer via a chain transfer reaction, and then introducing them into the branch chain [24]. Graft copolymers often combine properties of the individual components. The functionalization of the polymer can be achieved by introducing specific functional groups onto the macromolecular RAFT reagent. This facilitates subsequent chemical modifications and reactions that further expand the range of applications of polymers [25].

In our previous work, cellulose derivatives with -C=C and -NH2 groups were prepared by esterification or etherification of -OH on cellulose [26–28]. The Schiff base reaction or substitution reaction is performed by introducing the active group -NH2 to introduce fluorophores and finally the identification site is introduced to synthesize cellulose-based fluorescent macromolecular probes. But using ionic liquids on a large scale is both expensive and difficult, and their non-volatile nature makes them challenging to recover.

In this article, a cellulose-based RAFT reagent was prepared for grafting MAA and fluorescent monomers onto it via the RAFT polymerization method. Prior to the grafting reactions, the macromolecular RAFT reagent, Cell-CTA, was dissolved in DMSO, and cellulose-based polymers were synthesized using AIBN as an initiator. The -COOH groups in MAA give cellulose-based co-polymers excellent water solubility. The incorporation of naphthalimide moieties provides these polymers with luminescence and recognition sites that selectively bind Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence quenching of macromolecular fluorescence probes by Fe3+ is utilized for the specific detection of Fe3+. The reason for the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe3+ is analyzed [29].

Microcrystalline cellulose was used, the viscosity-average degree of polymerization (DP) of cellulose materials measured using an Ubbelohde viscometer in NaOH+CO(NH2)2 aqueous solution was 12.1 × 104 g/mol. Thionyl chloride (SOCl2), 2-mercaptobenzothiazole, potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium Iodide (KI), 4-bromo-1, 8-naphthalene anhydride, allylamine hydrochloride, piperidine, cuprous iodide, potassium carbonate, 2-thiophenethiol, reduced iron powder, 2,2′-azobis(2-methylpropionitrile) (AIBN), methacrylic acid (MAA) were purchased from J&K China Chemical Ltd. The solvents used such as carbon disulfide (CS2), ethyl alcohol (EtOH), glacial acetic acid and dichloromethane (DCM), triethylamine (Et3N), hydrazine hydrate, ethanol (EtOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), ethyl acetate (EA), petroleum ether (PE), acetone, acetonitrile (MeCN), tetrahydrofuran (THF) and all metal ions (Na+, Al3+, Ag+, Pd2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Hg+, Mn2+, K+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Co2+) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. The Solvent in reaction were purified by distillation for further use.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained on Nicolet 6700 instruments. The test specimens were prepared by the KBr-disk method. 1H NMR were recorded on AVANCE III 400 MHz (Bruker) instrument. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was employed to obtain the molecular weight (MW), and MW distribution. Polymer Laboratories PL-GPC 50 Plus integrated GPC system from Wyatt Technology, USA was used. The elemental, content and binding energy were carried out using an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5300 ESCA). Mass spectra (ESI-HRMS) was taken by an Agilent QTOF LC/MS instrument. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) were performed on German STA449F3. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-5900PC spectrophotometer (Metash). Fluorescence spectra were tested on F97 Pro fluorescence spectrophotometer (Lengguang, China). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) data were collected on a Bruker D8 Advanced with DaVinci design instrument. Analysis of the content of metal elements using the instrument inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) 7700 (Agilent USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was determined by a JEM 2100 (JEOL, Japan).
The preparation of small molecules was shown in Scheme 1 (a). The fluorescent grafted cellulose copolymers were synthesized according to Scheme 1 (b).

Allylamine hydrochloride (30 mg, 0.3 mmol) in ethanol (4 mL), and another solution of triethylamine (44.6 μL), 4-bromo-1, 8-naphthalene anhydride (89 mg, 0.32 mmol) in 3 mL ethanol was prepared. And then, the two ethanol solution were mixed together. The mixture is heated for 4–5 h at 60 °C. After the reaction, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature. Then the mixture was extracted with DCM and deionized water. PE/EA (30:1, v:v) was used as eluent and purified by silica gel column chromatography. N1 in white solid form (75 mg, 0.24 mmol, 74 %), 1H NMR (Fig. S1 (a), 400 MHz, CDCl3), δ8.63 (d, 1H), 8.55 (d, 1H), 8.40 (d, 1H), 8.04 (d, 1H), 7.84 (t, 1H), 5.99 (d, 1H), 5.34 (d, 1H), 5.22 (d, 1H), 5.99 (m, 1H), 4.78 (d, 1H). Fig. S1 represents the chart displayed in the attachment.

N1 (30mg, 0.10 mmol), CuI (9.05 mg, 0.10 mmol), K2CO3 (14 mg, 0.05 mmol) and piperidine (50 μL, 0.46 mmol) were added to DMF and reacted at 90 °C for 10 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the mixture was extracted with DCM and deionized water. PE/EA (25:1) was used as eluent and purified by silica gel column chromatography. N1–1 in orange solid form (25 mg, 0.078 mmol, 82 %), 1H NMR (Fig. S1(b), 400 MHz, CDCl3), δ8.51 (d, 3H), 7.68 (t, 1H), 7.18 (d, 1H), 5.98 (m, 5H), 5.31 (d, 6H), 5.17 (d, 1H), 4.79 (d, 2H), 3.24 (s, 4H), 1.90 (s, 4H), 1.72 (s, 2H). ESI-MS (m/z): [m + H]+ calculated for C20H20N2O2: 321.1603 and found 321.1604 (Fig. S3).

N1-1 (30 mg, 0.10 mmol) reacted with 2-thiophene mercaptan (22 mg, 0.19 mmol) and triethylamine (66 μL, 0.475 mmol) at DMF at 80 °C in N2 atmosphere. After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and purified by silica gel column chromatography with PE/EA (20:1) as eluent. N1-2 with deep yellow solid form (20mg, 0.06 mmol, 60 %), 1H NMR (Fig. S1(c), 400 MHz, CDCl3), δ8.66 (d, 2H), 8.35 (d, 1H), 7.80 (t, 1H), 7.66 (d, 1H), 7.45 (d, 1H), 7.23 (d, 1H), 7.17 (t, 1H), 5.98 (m, 5H), 5.32 (d, 6H), 5.21 (d, 1H), 4.78 (s, 2H). ESI-MS (m/z): [m + H]+ calculated for C19H13NO2S2: 352.0466 and found 352.0463 (Fig. S4).

Firstly, allylamine hydrochloride (30 mg, 0.12 mol) was dissolved in 2 mL of ethanol. Then, 4-nitro-1,8-naphthalic anhydride (57 mg, 0.18 mol) was added to the in the above solution, and stirred at room temperature for 15 min. Finally, the resulting mixture was stirred at a temperature of 60 °C for 4 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, and the mixture was extracted with DCM and deionized water. N1-2 was obtained as a deep yellow solid (32 mg, 0.11 mmol, 63 %), 1H NMR (Fig. S1(d), 400 MHz, CDCl3), δ8.83 (d, 1H), 8.73 (d, 1H), 8.68 (d, 1H), 8.41 (t, 1H), 8.01 (d, 1H), 5.97 (m, 1H), 5.37 (d, 1H), 5.21 (d, 2H).

N21 (30 mg, 0.10 mmol) was reacted with reduced iron powder (4 eq), water and acetic acid in a ratio of 2:1 as solvent at 60 °C for 8 h. After the reaction, the mixed solution was adjusted to pH = 7 with ammonia water. The coarse product was obtained by centrifugation. The coarse product was purified to obtain yellow solid by silica gel column chromatography. N2-2 was obtained in yellow solid form (18 mg, 0.072 mmol, 76 %), 1H NMR (Fig. S1(e), 400 MHz, DMSO), δ8.59 (d, 1H), 8.39 (d, 1H), 8.16 (d, 1H), 7.63 (t, 1H) 7.44 (d, 2H), 6.84 (d, H), 5.88 (m, 1H), 5.08 (d, 2H), 5.04 (d, 1H), 4.60 (s, 1H). ESI-MS (m/z): [m + H]+ computation for C15H12N2O2: 253.0977, found 253.0976 (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cell-Cl. (the primary hydroxyl group on cellulose is replaced by Cl)
ce:section.ce:para: Cell-Cl was prepared according to the reported method [30]. Cellulose powder (100 mg) was suspended in 3 mL of DMF at 353 K for about 8 h. The exothermic reaction was initiated by the gradual addition of SOCl2 (350 μL) to the pre-suspended heterogeneous solution. Upon introduction of SOCl2, a rapid color change from white to brown occurred, indicating initiation of reaction which continued for an additional 4 h at 353 K. As the reaction progressed, the mixture gradually darkened in color until completion. The pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by diluted NH4OH, and then a large amount of brown solid was precipitated. The solid was washed with deionized water for three times, then was separated by centrifuge. The solid was put into a vacuum drying oven and was dried at room temperature to obtain the products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cell-CTA. (In Cell-Cl, Cl is replaced by 2-methylbenzo[d]thiazole)
ce:section.ce:para: 2-mercaptobenzothiazole (30 mg) was added to the pre-stirred suspensions of KOH and DMSO and was further stirred to obtain a homogeneous mixture. The CS2 was added slowly to the mixture solution; the color of the solution immediately changed to yellow. The reaction continued at room temperature for about 10 h, followed by Cell-Cl (100 mg) at 90 °C for 12 h. The color of resulting mixture was brown. DCM was added to the mixture solution, and the flocculent solid was separate out. The product was washed with ethanol and deionized water for three times and was separated by centrifuge. Finally, the product was dried by vacuum at room temperature and the final product was a brown solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cellulose-based fluorescence probes. (CP1, CP2, CP3)
ce:section.ce:para: Cell-CTA (100 mg) was stirred at 80 °C until it was completely dissolved in DMSO, and the solution turned brown. Then, fluorescent small molecules N1–1 (30 mg, 0.10 mmol) was added into the solution until they were completely dissolved into DMSO, and the solution changed from brown to orange yellow, light yellow and bright yellow respectively. AIBN (4 mg) and MAA (200 μL) were dissolved in 1 mL ethanol, then was added to the above three different solutions respectively. RAFT polymerization was continued in N2 atmosphere at 80 °C and for 48 h, the polymerization was complete. After ultrasonic washing with THF and DCM for three times, the MAA and fluorescent small molecules that did not participate in the reaction were completely washed away. The final products were vacuum dried at room temperature. Finally, CP1 (orange yellow solid) was obtained. By using the same method, CP2 (light yellow solid) and CP3 (bright yellow solid) were obtained, respectively.

The cellulose derivatives Cell-Cl and Cell-CTA were characterized by 1H NMR, FTIR and XRD. The structure of the cellulose probes (CP1, CP2, CP3) was characterized by 1H NMR, FTIR, XPS, GPC and XRD. The crystallinity index, reactivity and surface morphology of untreated and chemically modified fibers were investigated by XRD, FTIR TEM, respectively. The thermal stability of the cellulose was analyzed by TGA and DSC. The fluorescence characteristics of the probes were further analyzed by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The structure and properties of naphthalimide fluorescent small molecules are analyzed in materials.

1H NMR of cellulose derivatives is shown in Fig. 1 . DMSO‑d 6 is used as the deuterium reagents for 1H NMR tests. The chemical shifts of the solvent peak and water peak in 1H NMR are 2.50 ppm and 3.30 ppm, respectively.

For cellulose derivatives Cell-Cl, Cell-CTA and cellulose-based polymers CP1, CP2 and CP3, the chemical shift from 2.47 ppm to 4.47 ppm belongs to H1- H5 of each glucose unit of cellulose [31]. For Cell-CTA (Fig. 1(b)), the chemical shift of the new peaks from 7.44 ppm to 8.18 ppm are attributed to H7-H10 on the benzene ring, which proves that Cell-CTA has been successfully synthesized. For CP1 (Fig. 1(c)), CP2 (Fig. 1(d)) and CP3 (Fig. 1(e)), the chemical shift of H14 at 12.31 ppm is attributed to the -COOH, which confirms the successful introduction of MAA. The new peaks observed at H15-H22 in CP1 were attributed to the successful introduction of N1-1. Similarly, the appearance of new peaks at H15-H20 in CP2 confirmed the successful introduction of N1-2. The emergence of a new peak at H15-H19 in CP3 additionally suggests the successful introduction of N2-2. The results demonstrate the successful grafting of MAA and fluorescent molecules onto cellulose through RAFT polymerization.

Cell-CTA is used as macromolecular RAFT reagent. The substitution degrees of Cell-CTA, CP1, CP2 and CP3 were calculated by 1H NMR [32]. By computation, DSCell-CTA = DSCP1 = DSCP2 = DSCP3 = 0.40.

The FTIR spectra of small molecules and cellulose-based polymers are shown in Fig. S2. From Fig. S2(a), N0 displays a distinct stretching vibration peak at 1023 cm−1, which corresponds to the O-C-O bond. The CN peak at 1230 cm−1 is observed in N1, N1-1, and N1-2. The band at 1660–1670 cm−1 represents both CC and CO bonds. There is a peak located at the original position of the CO bond, which was previously identified as 1730 cm−1. In comparison to N0, the disappearance of the O-C-O peak indicates a transformation from O-C-O to CN. The successful synthesis of N1, N1-1, and N1-2 has been accomplished. In comparison to N2-1, a new peak at 3459cm−1 appears, indicating that NH2 has been successfully introduced into N2-2. From Fig. S2(b), C0 (original cellulose) and cellulose derivatives Cell-Cl, Cell-CTA, cellulose-based polymers have obvious OH stretching vibration peaks at 1400 cm−1 [33]. Compared to the C0, the new peak at 720 cm−1 was attributed to CCl, confirming that Cell-Cl had been successfully prepared. The significant decrease in the peak at 720 cm−1 in Cell-CTA after reaction suggests the involvement of CCl in the chemical reaction. The new absorption peak at 1660 cm−1 was attributed to CN, confirming that Cell-CTA had been successfully synthesized. In solids, dimers are formed due to carboxylic acids. The new absorption peak at 1700 cm−1 is attributed to CO, which proves that MAA was introduced into cellulose by Cell-CTA. Additionally, the newly appeared stretching vibration peaks at 1260 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1 represent CS and CS respectively, thereby confirming the successful synthesis of cellulose-based polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular weight and molecular weight distribution test
ce:section.ce:para: The cellulose sample's original molecular weight was measured using viscometer and found to be 12.1 × 104 g/mol. The molecular weight and PDI of the cellulose graft copolymers CP1, CP2, CP3 were determined by gel permeation chromatography (GPC), as shown in Table 1 . gel permeation chromatography of molecular weight and distribution of CP1, CP2 and CP3 were showed in Fig. S6. The fluorescent probes obtained through RAFT polymerization reached the macromolecular level, with a numerical average molecular weight of over 1 × 104 g/mol. The PDI of cellulose probes CP1, CP2 and CP3 were 1.76, 2.08 and 2.31, respectively. A narrow PDI and a large molecular weight indicate that the macromolecule RAFT reagent can generate free radicals on the main chain of the polymer through a chain transfer reaction. Then, MAA and fluorescent molecules are introduced for polymerization reaction, resulting in a relatively uniform distribution of cellulose-based polymers. The results show that RAFT polymerization is more controllable than active polymerization, which can control molecular weight and have narrow PDI.
The X-ray diffraction (XRD) of cellulose derivatives Cell-Cl, Cell-CTA and the cellulose-based polymers are shown in Fig. 2 .

C0 has two characteristic peaks at 2θ = 14.8° and 2θ = 22.5°, corresponding to its crystalline structure [34]. Compared with C0, the peak strength of Cell-Cl, Cell-CTA, CP1, CP2 and CP3 at 2θ = 22.5° is significantly decreased and showed a weak single peak. This indicates that the modification of cellulose leads to a decrease in its crystallinity due to changes in its chemical structure. For Cell-Cl, the addition of the new chemical bond Cl destroys the regularity of cellulose structure and breaks the hydrogen bonds, resulting in a significant reduction in peak strength at 2θ = 22.5° and indicating a decrease in crystallinity. For Cell-CTA, a new diffraction peak appears in cellulose due to the addition of a new chemical bond benzothiazole. Polymethacrylate (PMAA) has wide diffraction peaks at 2θ = 15.45° and 31.22° [35], This may be due to the grafting of MAA on Cell-CTA, so the diffraction peak of PMAA at 2θ = 31.22° changes to 2θ = 40.47°. So, the graft of cellulose and PMAA results in the change of the semi-crystalline structure of PMAA matrix. As a result, the crystallinity of cellulose decreased significantly, and the solubility of cellulose was improved.
Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) are used to analyze a series of thermal property parameters for cellulose derivatives and cellulose-based polymers.

The glass transition temperature (Tg) of C0 is 220 °C [36]. According to Table 2 , compared to C0, the Tg of cellulose derivatives shows a slight reduction. Cellulose-based polymers have similar structures, except for the different fluorescent monomers, therefore, their Tg values are approximately the same. It proves that changes in cellulose structure can destroy hydrogen bonds and result in lower crystallinity. Additionally, there is a significant decrease in cellulose crystallinity, which leads to a reduction in its Tg.

According to the TGA in Fig. 3(b), In the first stage, the weight loss curve is smooth, mainly due to the loss of adsorbed water [37]. By obtaining the DTG curve from the TG curve as shown in Fig. S10, the maximum decomposition temperature can be determined and presented in Table 2. The results indicate that the addition of the unstable Cl chemical bond promotes the degradation of Cell-Cl and reduces its thermal stability. However, when benzothiazole replaces Cl, the thermal stability of Cell-CTA is enhanced. This demonstrates the introduction of a new chemically bonded. Due to the wide range of chemical bond activity in polymers, cellulose polymers almost all begin thermal degradation at 190 °C and undergo thermal degradation over a wider temperature range (190–456 °C). Compared to C0, polymers have much poorer thermal stability. The decrease in their thermal stability is mainly due to the introduction of other groups that break the intramolecular and intermolecular bonds of cellulose. When temperatures above 400 °C, almost all polymers are pyrolyzed with very low solid residues [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solubility test of cellulose-based fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The solubility of polymers was analyzed, and different proportions of Cell-CTA, MAA and small molecule fluorescent monomers were selected for RAFT polymerization with the same reaction conditions. Different products were obtained in varying proportions by adjusting the ratio of MAA and Cell-CTA to regulate the solubility of cellulose polymers. The solubility Table 3 shows that cellulose-based polymers are soluble in both water and organic solvents. The higher the proportion of MAA, the better the solubility of cellulose polymer in water. Therefore, adjusting the proportion of MAA can improve the solubility of cellulose. Consequently, its application value in aqueous solutions can be realized.

The CP1, CP2, and CP3 samples were individually dissolved in deionized water and subsequently centrifuged. The resulting supernatant was collected for subsequent analysis using dynamic light scattering (DLS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The original cellulose showed rod-like structure under TEM [38]. According to Fig. 4 , CP1 appeared spherical, this is probably due to the COOH dissociates in water and becomes charged; the charged species repel each other, resulting in a spherical shape [39]. CP2 and CP3 show a reunion phenomenon, which proves that they have good water solubility. In addition, DLS analysis was conducted according to Table S1, which proved that CP1, CP2 and CP3 could be dissolved in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selective recognition ability of cellulose-based fluorescent probes, we tested 19 common metal ions. Cellulose polymers have good solubility in DMSO, and all tests were conducted in a mixed solution of DMSO and H2O (DMSO: H2O = 1:5, v:v). The test data is shown in Figs. 5 and S7. The probes exhibit particularly high selectivity for Fe3+ under the same concentration conditions. As shown in the inserted pictures, a significant fluorescence quenching phenomenon was observed for the three macromolecular probes under UV light upon addition of 30 μL Fe3+ (10−2 mol/L). The fluorescence quenching efficiencies of CP1, CP2, and CP3 are 46 %, 49 %, and 81 % respectively. Among them, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon of CP3 is the most pronounced. The results indicate that the probes possess excellent fluorescence properties and can effectively differentiate Fe3+ from other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ion concentration on fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the effect of different concentrations of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of cellulose fluorescence probes, increasing amounts of Fe3+ were added to the probe from 0 μL to 100 μL. The mixture was thoroughly contacted, and the resulting fluorescence intensity was measured. Figs. 6a–c and S8 show that the fluorescence intensity at the maximum absorption wavelength gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration. Fig. 6e–f indicate that the fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength is linearly correlated with Fe3+ concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) of probes are calculated.
(2)
LOD = 3σ / K

In formula (2), σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence value of the blank cellulose-based fluorescence probes solution, and K is the slope of the standard curve. The LOD values of CP1, CP2 and CP3 are 0.66 μM, 0.90 μM and 0.48 μM, respectively. Based on Fig. S15, the detection limit of N2–2 is calculated to be 0.29 μM. In comparison with small molecules in this paper, the macromolecular probe did not show a significant change in LOD. Small molecule probes are capable of detecting metal ions with high sensitivity, but they often lack the ability to remove these ions from solution [39]. In contrast, macromolecular fluorescent probes can be precipitated and separated in the presence of Fe3+ ions, offering distinct advantages for identifying and isolating Fe3+ [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-interference study of probes in the presence of other metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of other metal ions on the recognition of Fe3+ by cellulose-based fluorescent probes was investigated. Upon addition of an equimolar amount of other metal ions to the probes, no significant alteration in the quenching effect was observed. From Fig. 7a–c, it is evident that there is no significant change in fluorescence intensity, indicating that other metal ions do not significantly interfere with the ability of the fluorescence probes to recognize Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic response experiment of cellulose-based fluorescent probes to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: To discuss whether the fluorescence probes can be recycled, 30 μL of Fe3+ was first added to the probes, followed by the addition of 30 μL of EDTA (10−2 mol/L). EDTA can complex with the macromolecular probe and compete with Fe3+ in the cycle [41]. As shown in Fig. S9, The CP1 and CP3 can undergo three cycles of recycling, whereas the CP2 is not recyclable. The recycling process has yielded significant environmental benefits.

After combining the fluorescence probe with Fe3+, the detection mechanism was analyzed by FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra. Considering the similar structure of cellulose-based polymers, CP1 was selected to analyze the changes after binding Fe3+. CP1 powder (15 mg) was dispersed in 4–5 mL deionized water, and then 100 μL Fe3+ solution (10−2 mol/L) was added to the mixed liquids. The Fe3+ solution was fully ultrasonic contacted with the macromolecular fluorescence probe by ultrasonic machine, and then washed with water and ethanol for three times, finally centrifuged to obtain the solid precipitate. The solid was our desired sample, which should be vacuum-dried at room temperature before testing.

Using Figs. S12–14, calculate the energy levels and frontier molecular orbitals (HOMO and LUMO) of the probes. According to Table 4 from electrochemical analysis, a negative change in Gibbs free energy (ΔG < 0) indicates thermodynamic spontaneity.

From Fig. 8 (a), For CP1, 1180 ppm correspond to CN stretching vibration peak. For CP1 + Fe3+, the CN at 1066 ppm occurred blue shift in the FTIR spectrum, this may be due to the metal ion Fe3+ binding at CN. So, it is preliminarily speculated that CN is the recognition site.

The N atom, characterized by its possession of lone pair electrons and small atomic volume, exhibits a robust bond and high affinity towards metal ions. Within the functional group of the macromolecular fluorescent probe CP1, the N atom forms a coordination covalent bond with Fe3+ ions, leading to the formation of a structurally stable chelate. According to the experimental plot of Job's experiment in Fig. S11, it can be inferred that the binding ratio between Fe3+ and small molecule N1-1 is 2:1. Consequently, each Fe3+ ion binds to two macromolecular fluorescent probes CP1, as depicted in Fig. 8. The adsorption of Fe3+ ion onto the cellulose macromolecular fluorescent probe is possible; however, they were completely eliminated through thorough ultrasonic washing with ethanol. The energy peak of the Fe3+ ion can still be detected at 709 eV in XPS in Fig. 8(b), indicating that the fluorescence quenching of CP1 is attributed to chemical complexation of CN chemical bonds rather than physical adsorption. According to Fig. S13, the ultraviolet wavelength and peak shape of CP1 have changed due to the complexation of Fe3+ with CN in CP1. New complexes are formed, and this complex formation may result in the transfer of electrons from nitrogen atoms in the naphthalimide ring to three-dimensional orbitals partially filled by Fe3+ ion. Therefore, a non-radiative electron or energy transfer process occurs that quenches the fluorescence of the complex [42]. The fluorescence intensity of the system that added EDTA can be effectively restored to (Fig. S13), indicating that the interaction between Fe3+ and EDTA is significantly stronger than CP1 [43], and the formation of new complexes does not emit light in the actual detected spectrum, which is referred to as static quenching phenomenon.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of Fe3+ by cellulose-based polymer in pure water
ce:section.ce:para: Instrument inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to detect the concentration of Fe3+ in aqueous solution to calculate the ability of CP1, CP2 and CP3 to adsorb Fe3+ in water. The results are shown in Table 5 . According to the results, all probes reached adsorption equilibrium in about 10 min. Finally, the removal efficiency of the macromolecular fluorescent probe was calculated.

The removal efficiency (Re) and the adsorption capacity (Qe) of the probes for Fe3+ were calculated by the following formulas (5) and (6):
(5)
R e
=
C 0
− C
/ C · 100 %
(6)
Q e
=
C 0
− C
V / m
C0 and C represent the initial concentration and equilibrium concentration of Fe3+, V represents the volume of Fe3+ solution, and m represents the mass of probes as adsorbent [44].

As can be seen from Table 5, the cellulose-based fluorescent probes have a certain adsorption capacity for Fe3+ in aqueous solution, which may be due to the fact that the -OH and -COOH functional groups of cellulose provide adsorption sites and form chemical bonds with Fe3+ [45]. The Removal efficiency % (Re) of CP3 is the highest, which may be because NH2 provides the adsorption site and forms a complex with Fe3+, so the adsorption efficiency of Fe3+ is improved [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of cellulose-based fluorescent probe and fluorescent small molecule probe with other reported probes
ce:section.ce:para: We refer to the fluorescent probes that have been reported in recent years, compare them with those in this paper, and summarize Table 6 . In addition to improving the limit of detection (LOD) compared to previous studies, it significantly enhances the water solubility of the cellulose-based probe. The ability to detect Fe3+ in pure water represents a remarkable advantage over some of other researchers' works. This feature makes it highly effective for identifying and separating Fe3+ within an aqueous environment.

In conclusion, cellulose-based polymeric fluorescent probes were synthesized by grafting long-chain PMAA and fluorescent molecules as branched chains onto the cellulose main chain. Due to the excellent water solubility of PMAA, we successfully synthesized water-soluble and organic-soluble (DMSO) cellulose fluorescent probes using Cell-CTA through RAFT polymerization. The cellulose-based fluorescent probes prepared in this way can specifically identify Fe3+ with low LOD. The detection limits for Fe3+ of CP1, CP2, and CP3 are 0.66 μM, 0.90 μM, and 0.48 μM respectively. Furthermore, the removal efficiency (Re) of probe CP3 for Fe3+ in pure water reached 78 %. The RAFT polymerization technique offers a viable approach to make cellulose-based graft functional copolymers soluble in both aqueous and organic solvents, such as DMSO, thereby expanding the range of their potential applications.

Yanqin Ma designed the experiment, conducted the testing and analysis, and wrote the original draft. Xinjian Cheng co-designed the experiments, assisted with testing. and data analysis., supervised the project, and contributed to writing, reviewing, and editing.
